coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent carbon dots derived from shikimic acid for ultra-high sensitive detection and efficient removal of sulfasalazine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Anzhang
Ju, Ping
Zhang, Qingxiang
Zhang, Guixue
Lu, Wenhui
Jiang, Long
Qu, Fengli
Zhang, Ensheng
coredata.dc:description: The extensive use of sulfasalazine (SSZ) poses potential threats to human health and ecosystems. Therefore, sensitive detection and simultaneous efficient removal of sulfasalazine is greatly desirable. Meanwhile, shikimic acid, a natural product obtained from Chinese star anise is a promising precursor for carbon dots construction due to its unique structure. Taking the above into account, in this work, novel fluorescent carbon dots (SA-CDs) for sensitive detection and highly efficient removal of sulfasalazine have been developed. SA-CDs were facilely obtained from shikimic acid by using one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. The structure and composition of SA-CDs were fully characterized with TEM, PXRD, FT-IR, DLS and XPS tests. Strong fluorescent emission centered at 428 nm was recorded for SA-CDs, enabling the detection of SSZ. Quantitatively speaking, SA-CDs can selectively detection SSZ amount 17 antibiotics with an experimental limit of detection (LOD) of 100 nM and a calculated LOD of 60 pM. Adsorption experiments suggested that chemical monolayer adsorption predominates in the adsorption process, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 22.67 mg/g. Aggregations formed during detection enable the simultaneous removal of SSZ upon centrifugation. Moreover, SA-CDs have been successfully applied to the detection of SSZ in real samples, including river water, soil, tea and eggs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Shikimic acid
Carbon dots
SSZ detection
SSZ removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Method for fluorescent measurement of SSZ
Mechanism for the detection and removal of SSZ
Analysis of SSZ in actual samples

All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of SA-CDs: (a) HR-TEM image of SA-CDs; (b) DLS test for SA-CDs; (c) The XRD patterns of SA-CDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of SA and SA-CDs; (e) XPS spectrum of SA-CDs; High resolution XPS of (f) C1s; (g) O1s; (h) C/O ratio of shikimic acid and SA-CDs.

Spectral characterization of SA-CDs: (a) The excitation/emission fluorescence contour plots of SA-CDs; (b) The normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of SA-CDs; (c) Time-resolved emission-decay curves of SA-CDs; (d) Fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs in Tris-HCl (10 mmol) buffer with different pH values (λex = 340 nm, λem = 428 nm, n = 3); (e) Fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 428 nm, n = 3) stored for different times.

Selectivity and sensitivity study of SA-CDs: (a) Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs in the presence of 50 μM various analytes (Inset: the photograph of the above SA-CDs in the presence of 50 μM various analytes under a 365 nm UV-lamp); (b) Response time of SA-CDs in SSZ detection (λex = 340 nm, λem = 430 nm); (c) Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs in the presence of 1 μM-50 μM SSZ; (d) Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs in the presence of 0–1000 nM SSZ; (e) Linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 430 nm, n = 3) and SSZ concentrations; (f) The fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 430 nm) in the presence of other analytes.

Adsorption and removal of SSZ: (a) The adsorption efficiency of SA-CDs on different oscillation time (0–40 min); (b) The Pseudo-second-order kinetic and (c) Pseudo-first-order kinetic isotherm fittings for SSZ on SA-CDs; (d) Effect of the SSZ concentration on the adsorption and the corresponding forms of Langmuir (e) and Freundlich (f) model.

Mechanism study of the detection and removal of SSZ: (a) The fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of SA-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of SSZ; (b) Time-resolved emission-decay curves of SA-CDs before and after treated with SSZ; (c) Zeta potential of SA-CDs before and after treated with SSZ (n = 3); (d)The DLS test of SA-CDs after treated with SSZ; (e) XPS spectrum of SA-CDs after treated with SSZ; High resolution XPS of SA-CDs after treated with SSZ, (f) C1s, (g) N1s, (h) O1s, (i) S2p; (j) A schematic diagram of the detection and removal mechanism.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis and applications of SA-CDs.
Different parameters of model fitting analysis.
Results for the detection of SSZ in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent carbon dots derived from shikimic acid for ultra-high sensitive detection and efficient removal of sulfasalazine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instrumental Analysis & Research Center, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instrumental Analysis & Research Center, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The extensive use of sulfasalazine (SSZ) poses potential threats to human health and ecosystems. Therefore, sensitive detection and simultaneous efficient removal of sulfasalazine is greatly desirable. Meanwhile, shikimic acid, a natural product obtained from Chinese star anise is a promising precursor for carbon dots construction due to its unique structure. Taking the above into account, in this work, novel fluorescent carbon dots (SA-CDs) for sensitive detection and highly efficient removal of sulfasalazine have been developed. SA-CDs were facilely obtained from shikimic acid by using one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. The structure and composition of SA-CDs were fully characterized with TEM, PXRD, FT-IR, DLS and XPS tests. Strong fluorescent emission centered at 428 nm was recorded for SA-CDs, enabling the detection of SSZ. Quantitatively speaking, SA-CDs can selectively detection SSZ amount 17 antibiotics with an experimental limit of detection (LOD) of 100 nM and a calculated LOD of 60 pM. Adsorption experiments suggested that chemical monolayer adsorption predominates in the adsorption process, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 22.67 mg/g. Aggregations formed during detection enable the simultaneous removal of SSZ upon centrifugation. Moreover, SA-CDs have been successfully applied to the detection of SSZ in real samples, including river water, soil, tea and eggs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the issue of antibiotic residues [1]. Sulfasalazine (SSZ), a sulfonamide antibiotic, is commonly employed to treat inflammatory bowel disease and ankylosing spondylitis [2]. However, due to its good affordability, SSZ was also excessive used as a feed additive or in animal disease treatment [3], resulting in SSZ residual in water sources and consumable products. The abuse of SSZ contributes to its spread in the food chain and environmental ecosystems, which subsequently may lead to the presence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria that adversely affect the environment and human health [4]. Therefore, the development of a highly effective method for detecting SSZ residues in water, beverages and food is imperative. To date, various methods including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [5], electrochemical methods [6], solid-phase extraction liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry [7], and fluorescent probes [8] have been developed for detection of SSZ. Among these methods, fluorescent probes are gaining increasing attention due to their intriguing characteristics such as good selectivity, high sensitivity and user-friendliness [9]. Although, a number of fluorescent probes including coordination compounds [10], quantum dots [11] and metal–organic frameworks [12] have been developed, fluorescent probe that could detect and simultaneous removal of SSZ is still rare.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nano-material possessing excellent optical properties. Their unique structure and composition enable them to engage with specific substances on a molecular level, thereby enhancing signal amplification and enabling precise quantitative analysis [13]. Besides, research on multifunctional carbon dots and its variants for theranostic applications has made great progress [14,15]. Since the first discovery of carbon quantum dots in 2004, they have been widely applied in fluorescence detection [16]. Pioneering work on the development of fluorescent CDs for antibiotic detection has demonstrated their high feasibility and gave us great inspiration [17]. Moreover, through the functionalization of carbon dots with specific ligands or receptors, scientists have successfully engineered sophisticated biosensors that are capable of detecting trace concentrations of antibiotics in various biological samples [18]. Shikimic acid (SA, (3R, 4S, 5R)-(−)-3, 4, 5-trihydroxy-1-cyclohexenecarboxylic acid) is a natural product that could be obtained sustainably in large quantity from Chinese star anise [19]. The unique structure of shikimic acid, featuring multi-hydroxyl groups that facilitate the aromatization process, renders it a promising precursor for the construction of carbon dots. However, to the best of our knowledge, fluorescent CDs that are constructed from SA have not been revealed thus far.

As a new progress of our continuous works on the utilization of shikimic acid [20–22], herein, the first carbon dots (SA-CDs) derived from shikimic acid are revealed for the sensitive detection and highly efficient removal of sulfasalazine (Scheme 1 and Table S1). SA-CDs are synthesized from shikimic acid using one-pot hydrothermal method and exhibit strong bright blue fluorescent emission. Seventeen antibiotics were screened, and only SSZ displayed remarkable fluorescent quenching response to SA-CDs. Upon treatment with SSZ, the fluorescent of SA-CDs was instantaneously quenched and a good linear response was recorded. Quantitatively speaking, SA-CDs exhibited a good experimental LOD of 100 nM and an extremely high theoretical LOD of 60 pM for SSZ. Moreover, obvious aggregations were observed in the sensing of SSZ, which could facilitate the removal of SSZ. Mechanism studies revealed that hydrogen bonds might be formed between SA-CDs and SSZ, playing a key role in the detection and adsorption processes. Additionally, adsorption experiments suggested that chemical monolayer adsorption predominates in the adsorption process. Most importantly, fluorescent detection of SSZ in natural water, soil, tea, and eggs was performed using SA-CDs.

All the reagents utilized in the experiments were commercially available and used without further purification. The abbreviations of the reagents are listed as follows: Shikimic acid (SA), Sulfathiazole (STZ), Sulfapyridine (SPD), Phthalylsulfathiazole (PSZ), Sulfameter (SM), Sulfadiazine Silver (SDS), Sulfafurazole (SFZ), Sulfamethoxazole (SMX), Sulfamethazine (SMZ), Sulfaguanidine (SGD), Sulfacetamide (S-A), Sulfadiazine (SDZ), Sulfadoxine (SD), 3,5-Dichlorosulfanilamide (DSA), Bromobenzenesulfonamide (BBS), Benzenemethane sulfonamide (BMSA), Chloramphenicol (CPL), Sulfasalazine (SSZ).

Fluorescent spectra were recorded on an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. FT-IR spectra were tested using a Shimadzu IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer. The microstructure of the materials was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOL JEM-2100PLUS instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were conducted using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument from Malvern, UK. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi instrument from the USA.

The synthetic conditions for SA-CDs were optimized and are displayed in Fig. S1. The proposed synthesis of carbon dots (SA-CDs) from shikimic acid was achieved by using the following hydrothermal method. Specifically, shikimic acid (0.8 g, 4.6 mmol) was added to a PTFE reactor containing 10 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 1 min to form a clear solution. The hydrothermal reactors were then heated in an oven at 180 °C for 18 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was passed through a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was further purified by membrane filtration and a light brown aqueous solution of carbon dots was obtained. The powder of SA-CDs could be obtained by freeze-drying or evaporating the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method for fluorescent measurement of SSZ
ce:section.ce:para:
The stock solution of SA-CDs with a concentration of 3.0 mg/mL was prepared by dispersing SA-CDs powder into pure water with ultrasonication. 200 μL of SA-CDs stock solution (3.0 mg/mL) was used in the fluorescent detection and the final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 2 mL with distilled water, ensuring that the final concentration of analytes was within the range of 0–50 μM. Then, the fluorescent spectra of these SA-CDs solutions were recorded upon excitation at 340 nm. The interference experiments were performed by recording the fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs solutions in the presence of both SSZ (50 μM) and other antibiotics (50 μM). Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs solution were recorded with gradually increasing SSZ concentration (0.1 μM–50 μM) to perform the fluorescent titration. All experiments were performed in parallel three times (n = 3). The error bar was calculated with the formula
SD =
Σ
F -
F ¯
2
n - 1
n = 3
.

The adsorption and removal performance of SA-CDs on SSZ were evaluated using the following methods. The adsorption kinetics was studied by adding 0.3 g SA-CDs to a SSZ solution (100 mL, 5 mg/L) with different incubation times. After shaking at room temperature for a certain period of time, the mixture was centrifuged, and the residuary SSZ in the liquid supernatant was determined to study the adsorption kinetics. The adsorption capacity was studied by adding 0.3 g SA-CDs to SSZ solutions with different initial concentrations. The equilibrium adsorption capacity Qe (mg/g) was calculated using the following Eq. (1) [23]
(1)
Q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
) V
m

The adsorption capacity Qt (mg/g) at any time t (min) was calculated using the following Eq. (2)
(2)
Q t
=
(
C 0
-
C t
) V
m
where C0 (mg/L) is the initial SSZ concentration of the solution; Ce and Ct (mg/L) are the equilibrium concentration of the adsorbed solution and the concentration of residuary SSZ in the sample solution at time t, respectively; V (L) is the volume of the solution; m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent; Q t (mg/g) and Q e (mg/g) represent the adsorption capacity at any time t (min) and the equilibrium adsorption capacity [23]. The pseudo-first-order (PFO) [24,25] and pseudo-second-order (PSO) [26] models were described by Eq. (3) and Eq. (4)
(3)
ln
(
Q e
-
Q t
)

t ,
(4)

1

2
,
where k1 (1/min) is the equilibrium rate constant of the pseudo first-order sorption and k2 (g/mg/min) is the equilibrium rate constant of the pseudo second-order sorption. The fitting equations of the Langmuir model and Freundlich model are shown in Eq. (5) and Eq. (6), respectively [27].
(5)
C e
Q e
=
1
Q m
K L
+
C e
Q m
,
(6)
ln
Q e
= ln
k F
+
1 n
ln
C e
.

To further investigate the feasibility of SA-CDs in real sample analysis, the recovery of actual samples was recorded. River water sample was collected from Si River in Qufu. The river water sample was centrifuged and further filtered by using a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove impurities before use. The soil sample was collected from local farm to prepare the soil leaching solution. In detail, 3 g soil sample was added to a beaker with 50 mL of acetonitrile. After ultrasonic dispersion and soaked for 2 h, the mixture was filtrated. The filtrate was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to obtain a homogeneous liquid supernatant. The obtained solution was evaporated to form a 5 mL concentrated solution which was used as the stock solution. Green tea samples (3.0 g) were brewed with 150 mL boiling water for 2 min. After that, pouring off the first tea water and brewing the tea with another 150 mL boiling water for 2 or 3 min again. When time is out, the secondary tea water was transferred into clean beaker and then filtered with 0.22 µm membranes and stored at 4 °C. And the tea water was diluted 10 times when finally tested. The egg samples (5 g) were subjected to extraction with 50 mL of acetonitrile containing 0.1 % acetic acid to eliminate lipids and proteins. The solution was centrifuged, and the resulting supernatant was evaporated until nearly dry before being reconstituted in water. All the pretreated samples were then analyzed by using SA-CDs. The spiked samples were created by adding the appropriate amount of SSZ to the pretreated samples and analyzed by using SA-CDs. Each experiment was repeated three times (n = 3). The error bar was calculated with the formula
SD =
Σ
F -
F
¯
2
n - 1
(n = 3).

The size, morphology, composition and valent states of SA-CDs are revealed with TEM, PXRD, DLS, XPS and FT-IR tests. HR-TEM image of SA-CDs is displayed in Fig. 1 a. According to the image, the particle size of SA-CDs is determined to be 2 nm–3 nm, which is consistent with the DLS test result (Fig. 1b) and indicates good dispersion. The lattice spacing of SA-CDs is determined to be 0.22 nm which can be assigned to the (100) plane of graphite carbon. There are four characteristic peaks of graphitic carbon and an amorphous peak at 23° could be observed from the X-ray diffraction spectrum of SA-CDs (Fig. 1c) [28]. XRD peaks around 13°–19° may be ascribed to the combination of the 004 graphite plane and other lattices with oxidation planes, suggesting that SA-CDs may have a non-ideal crystal structure. The diffraction peaks at around 25° and 43° are consistent with the known turbine layer carbon, representing crystal planes 002 and 100, respectively [29]. The spikes that occur at around 27° could be attributed to crystal plane 005 [30]. The FT-IR spectra of SA and SA-CDs are depicted in Fig. 1d. The characteristic stretching vibrations of OH at 3480 cm−1 and 3378 cm−1 are significantly reduced in SA-CDs compared with that in shikimic acid, indicating the dehydration reaction occurred in the synthesis of SA-CDs [31]. The new peaks at 3083 cm−1, 1500 cm−1–1600 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic C(sp2)H bond and the aromatic ring, respectively. These spectral characteristics reveal that dehydration-aromatization reaction occurred in the construction of SA-CDs [20–22].

In order to study the valence bond structure and the ratio between the elements of SA-CDs, the XPS test for SA-CDs was carried out. According to the full XPS spectrum of SA-CDs, there are two typical peaks at 284.4 eV and 531.2 eV (Fig. 1e), which can be ascribed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively. Therefore, SA-CDs were primarily composed of C and O. Moreover, according to the XPS test results, the content of elements C and O are 81.06 % and 18.94 %, respectively. Three constituent peaks at 283.9 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.3 eV are observed in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s, which could be attributed to the graphitic carbon characteristic peak, CO and CC/CC, respectively (Fig. 1f) [32,33]. Moreover, the two constituent peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.0 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s are ascribed to CO and CO, respectively. In addition, the C/O ratio of in SA-CDs remarkably increased compared to that in SA, further confirming the dehydration in the synthesis.

Initially, the optimized excitation and emission of SA-CDs were recorded before fluorescent measurements. A 3D contour map of the excitation/emission of SA-CDs was displayed in Fig. 2 a. According to the 3D contour map, the optimized excitation and emission of SA-CDs were 340 nm and 428 nm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption, fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of SA-CDs were recorded to further reveal the optical features of SA-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2b, strong absorption at 291 nm and 328 nm could be observed in the UV–vis absorption of SA-CDs, indicating there is a large conjugated system in the structure of SA-CDs. Moreover, the maximum fluorescent excitation and emission wavelengths of SA-CDs were consistent with that recorded in Fig. 2a. In addition, SA-CDs exhibit a fluorescent lifetime of 1.58 ns (Fig. 2c) and a 46.3 % fluorescence quantum yield that was calculated with reference to quinine sulfate [34].

In order to reveal the potential pH effect, fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer were recorded. Upon excitation at 340 nm, the fluorescent intensities of SA-CDs centered at 428 nm were displayed in Fig. 2d. As can be seen from Fig. 2d, SA-CDs exhibit weak fluorescence in strong acidic environments. However, remarkable fluorescent enhancement could be observed in neutral and alkaline environments. Thus, in order to exclude the pH influence, all the fluorescent sensing was carried out in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM). Moreover, the fluorescent stability of SA-CDs was revealed by continuously recording the fluorescence of SA-CDs for six weeks. As depicted in Fig. 2e, no obvious intensity change was observed for SA-CDs after being stored for six weeks, suggesting the good stability of SA-CDs.

The optimized probe concentration was first studied by recording the fluorescence of SA-CDs with different concentrations ranging from 0.05 mg/mL to 0.50 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. S2, SA-CDs with a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL displayed the strongest fluorescent emission, and this concentration were chosen for the following experiments. Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs in the presence of various analytes at 50 µM were recorded and depicted in Fig. 3 a. According to the results, only SSZ could remarkably quench the fluorescence of SA-CDs among the seventeen screened antibiotics (Fig. 3a and the inset picture), indicating the good selectivity of SA-CDs in antibiotics detection. The response time of SA-CDs to SSZ was recorded in Fig. 3b and an instantaneous response could be observed.

According to the selectivity experiments displayed in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of SA-CDs in the presence of 50 μΜ SSZ, calculated using the formula (F0-F)/F0 (where F0 represents the fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs in the absence of SSZ and F represents the fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs in the presence of 50 μΜ SSZ), is higher than 90 %. This result prompted us to conduct fluorescence titration experiments. Fluorescent spectra of SA-CDs (0.3 mg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of SSZ (0–50 µM and 0–1 µM) were recorded in Fig. 3c–d, all the samples were performed in triplicate. It is evident that the fluorescence intensity of SA-CDs gradually decreases with the increase in SSZ concentration. Noticeable fluorescence quenching can already be observed when the SSZ concentration is 100 nM, indicating the high sensitivity of SA-CDs in SSZ detection. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of SA-CDs and the SSZ concentration was observed in the range of 0 µM–1 µM, with a linear fitting correlation coefficient of 0.9981 (Fig. 3e). The detection limit (LOD) of SA-CDs for SSZ was calculated to be 60 pM based on the formula of LOD = 3σ/s, which is superior to previous reports (Table S1). Moreover, SA-CDs displayed good anti-interference performance in the fluorescent detection of SSZ. As displayed in Fig. 3f, remarkable fluorescent quenching could be observed for SA-CDs after treatment with 50 µM SSZ, regardless of the presence of other antibiotics. These results clearly indicate that the fluorescence quenching effect induced by SSZ would not be interfered with by other antibiotics.

Further investigations were conducted to explore the adsorption and removal capability of SA-CDs towards SSZ. The adsorption kinetics of SA-CDs towards SSZ were initially examined to understand the impact of adsorption time. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the adsorption rate initially increased rapidly due to the abundant adsorption sites on SA-CDs, followed by a decrease and eventual equilibrium within 40 min. To delve deeper into the adsorption kinetics, both the pseudo-first-order (Eq. (3)) and pseudo-second-order kinetic models (Eq. (4)) were employed. The fittings of the pseudo-second-order and pseudo-first-order kinetic isotherms for SSZ on SA-CDs are presented in Fig. 4b–c, with their respective kinetic parameters detailed in Table 1 . The adsorption kinetics of SA-CDs for SSZ demonstrated a better fit with the pseudo-second-order model, indicated by a higher correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9977), which highlights a chemisorption-dominated process between SSZ and the binding sites of SA-CDs [35,36].

The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were utilized to elucidate the adsorption mechanism. The impact of SSZ concentration was explored by examining the adsorption isotherm of SA-CDs (Fig. 4d). Data were fitted to the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, as depicted in Fig. 4e–f. The adsorption parameters are outlined in Table 1. Based on these parameters, a superior correlation coefficient for the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.9876) compared to the Freundlich model was observed, suggesting that the adsorption of SSZ by SA-CDs aligns more closely with the Langmuir model and monolayer absorption [37]. Furthermore, the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of SA-CDs for SSZ was determined to be 22.67 mg/g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection and removal of SSZ
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous reports, several mechanisms including aggregation induced quenching (ACQ) [38], internal filtering effects (IFE) [39], light-induced electron transfer (PET) [40], and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [41] were employed to illustrate the fluorescence quenching of CDs. In order to reveal the detection mechanism, fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of SA-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of SSZ were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, SSZ exhibits a broad absorption in the ultraviolet and visible light regions that well overlaps with the excitation spectrum of SA-CDs and partially overlapped with the emission spectrum of SA-CDs. Therefore, the excitation light for SA-CDs would be competitively absorbed and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from SA-CDs to SSZ occurs. Thus, the internal filtering effect (IFE) of SSZ could be employed to describe the fluorescent quenching of SA-CDs.

Moreover, fluorescent quenching mechanisms can be categorized into two main types: static quenching and dynamic quenching [42,43]. Dynamic quenching typically involves collisions between the quencher and the fluorophores in their excited states, while static quenching occurs when they are closely associated. Differentiating between these two mechanisms can be achieved by comparing the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores in the presence and absence of the analyte. If the fluorescence lifetime remains constant upon the introduction of an external analyte, the quenching process is considered static. Conversely, if the fluorescence lifetime changes following exposure to an external analyte, the quenching process is dynamic. Therefore, time-resolved emission-decay curves of SA-CDs before and after treatment with SSZ were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence lifetime of SA-CDs only slightly changed from 1.58 ns to 1.29 ns after treatment with SSZ, suggesting the static quenching is the dominant sensing mechanism.

According to the DLS test results, the addition of SSZ would cause a slight increase of the negative surface charge (Fig. 5c) and the aggregation of carbon dots (Fig. 5d vs Fig. 1b). To exclude the possibility of fluorescence quenching caused by carbon dots aggregation (ACQ), the real-time particle size and fluorescent intensity of SA-CDs after the addition of SSZ were tested (Fig. S3). It was found that the fluorescence of SA-CDs was immediately quenched after being treated with SSZ, but no obvious aggregation could be formed at this stage. Thus, the fluorescence quenching effect in carbon dots was not caused by the aggregation of particles. However, the aggregation of SA-CDs could facilitate the removal of SSZ after centrifugalization. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of SA-CDs could be attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE) of SSZ.

More evidence for the close association between SA-CDs and SSZ could be obtained from the XPS test of SA-CDs after treatment with SSZ. As depicted in the full XPS spectrum of SA-CDs in the absence of SSZ (Fig. 1d), only two characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s) and 531.2 eV (O 1s) were observed. After detection of SSZ, two new peaks at 164.0 eV (S 2p) and 398.4 eV (N 1s) were observed, and the oxygen content increased in the XPS spectra of SA-CDs, indicating the presence of SSZ in the aggregation (Fig. 5e). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 5f), there are five peaks at 283.9 eV (graphitic carbon), 284.6 eV (CC/CC), 285.6 eV (CO/CS), 286.5 eV (CN), and 289.4 eV (CO/CN). Moreover, three constituent peaks at 531.1 eV (SO), 531.9 eV (CO) and 532.8 eV (COH) in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s were observed (Fig. 5g). Compared with the CO, CO, COH bonds in pure SA-CDs, a slight decrease in binding energy was observed in the aggregation (Fig. 5g). Therefore, hydrogen bonds might have formed between SA-CDs and SSZ, resulting in an increase in the electron density of SA-CDs. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s and S 2p further confirms the chemical constitution of the aggregation. (The four-constituent peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.4 eV, 400.3 eV and 401.6 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s are ascribed to the pyridine N, CN/NN, CN/CS, and NH bond, respectively (Fig. 5h). The three constituent peaks at 167.2 eV, 167.7 eV and 168.5 eV are ascribed to the SO, ArS and NS bonds, respectively (Fig. 5i).

With the above results in hand, we can confirm that there is a close association between SA-CDs and SSZ. The fluorescence quenching could be ascribed to the IFE of SSZ and the FRET from SA-CDs to SSZ (Fig. 5j). The close association between SA-CDs and SSZ, with SSZ acting as the linker, resulted in the aggregation of SA-CDs. Accordingly, the aggregation of SA-CDs facilitated subsequent centrifugal separation of SSZ, as depicted in Fig. 5j.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SSZ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the feasibility of SA-CDs for detecting SSZ in real samples, the spiked recovery method was used to quantitatively assess SSZ in real samples, including river water, soil, tea and eggs. All the samples were pretreated according to the methods described in the experiment section (Section 2.5). The spiked samples were obtained by adding the appropriate amount of SSZ to the pretreated samples. All the samples were then analyzed with SA-CDs with three parallel experiments for each sample. The concentrations of SSZ in the samples were calculated using the linear fitting equations (Fig. 3e), and the results are shown in Table 2 . Notably, almost no SSZ was found in the blank samples. Recovery was calculated using the following equation: Recovery (%) = C1/C0 × 100 % [44]; where C1 represents the concentration of SSZ calculated according to calibration curve, C0 represents the concentration of SSZ added, and the calculation results were listed in Table 2. The recovery rates of SSZ in river water, soil, tea and eggs range from 100 % to 101.3 %, with the RSD less than 4.4 %. These results confirmed the good sensitivity and feasibility of SA-CDs in the analysis of trace SSZ in practical samples.

In conclusion, novel fluorescent carbon dots (SA-CDs) for sensitive detection and highly efficient removal of sulfasalazine have been developed as a new progression of our continuous work on the utilization of shikimic acid. SA-CDs were facile obtained from shikimic acid by using one-pot hydrothermal synthesis and fully characterized by TEM, PXRD, FT-IR, DLS and XPS. SA-CDs exhibit strong fluorescent emission centered at 428 nm upon excitation at 340 nm. Fluorescent sensing experiments demonstrate that SA-CDs can selectively response to SSZ among seventeen antibiotics with an experimental LOD of 100 nM and an extremely high theoretical LOD of 60 pM. Aggregations of SA-CDs would be formed after treatment with SSZ that could facilitate the removal of SSZ with centrifugation. A close association between SA-CDs and SSZ was confirmed, and hydrogen bonds might be formed which play a key role in the detection and adsorption process. Moreover, adsorption experiments suggested that chemical monolayer adsorption predominates in the adsorption process. Most importantly, SA-CDs have been successfully employed for detection of SSZ in natural water, soil, tea and eggs.

Anzhang Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Ping Ju: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Qingxiang Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Guixue Zhang: Formal analysis. Wenhui Lu: Investigation. Long Jiang: Resources, Conceptualization. Fengli Qu: Supervision, Formal analysis. Ensheng Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged the financial support from Qingchuang Science and Technology Project of Universities in Shandong Province, China (Grant No. 2021KJ070), projects ZR2023QB047, ZR2020QB156 supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science, National Natural Science Foundation of China (22007055).

ce:section.ce:para: All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112045.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
